en.extract:
The short-eared owl (Asio flammeus) is a widespread grassland species in the family Strigidae. Owls belonging to genus Asio are known as the eared owls, as they have tufts of feathers resembling mammalian ears. These "ear" tufts may or may not be visible. The short-eared owl will display its tufts when in a defensive pose, although its very short tufts are usually not visible. The short-eared owl is found in open country and grasslands.


Taxonomy
The short-eared owl was formally described in 1763 by the Lutheran bishop Erik Pontoppidan under the binomial name Strix flammea. The specific epithet is from the Latin flammeus meaning "flammulated" or "flame-coloured". This owl is now placed with seven other species in the genus Asio that was introduced by the French zoologist Mathurin Jacques Brisson in 1760.
Eleven subspecies are recognised:

A. f. flammeus – (Pontoppidan, 1763): the nominate subspecies, found in North America, Europe, North Africa and northern Asia
A. f. cubensis – Garrido, 2007: found in Cuba
A. f. domingensis – (Statius Müller, 1776): found on Hispaniola
A. f. portoricensis – Ridgway, 1882: found in Puerto Rico
A. f. bogotensis – Chapman, 1915: found in Colombia, Ecuador and northwestern Peru
A. f. galapagoensis – (Gould, 1837): found on the Galápagos Islands
A. f. pallidicaudus – Friedmann, 1949: found in Venezuela, Guyana and Suriname
A. f. suinda – (Vieillot, 1817): found from southern Peru and southern Brazil to Tierra del Fuego
A. f. sanfordi – Bangs, 1919: found on the Falkland Islands
A. f. sandwichensis – (A. Bloxam, 1827): Pueo or Hawaiian short-eared owl - found on the Hawaiian Islands
A. f. ponapensis – Mayr, 1933: found on Pohnpei in the Caroline Islands


Description
The short-eared owl is a medium-sized owl measuring 34–43 cm (13–17 in) in length and weighing 206–475 g (7.3–16.8 oz). It has large eyes, a big head, a short neck, and broad wings. Its bill is short, strong, hooked and black. Its plumage is mottled tawny to brown with a barred tail and wings. The upper breast is significantly streaked. Its flight is characteristically floppy due to its irregular wingbeats. The short-eared owl may also be described as "moth or bat-like" in flight. Wingspans range from 85 to 110 cm (33 to 43 in). Females are slightly larger than males. The yellow-orange eyes of A. flammeus are exaggerated by black rings encircling each eye, giving the appearance of them wearing mascara, and large, whitish disks of plumage surrounding the eyes like a mask.


Calls
Short-eared owls have a scratchy bark-like call. Raspy waowk, waowk, waowk or toot-toot-toot-toot-toot sounds are common. A loud eeee-yerp is also heard on breeding grounds. However, short-eared owls are silent on the wintering grounds.


Separation from the long-eared owl
Through much of its range, short-eared owls occurs with the similar-looking long-eared owl. At rest, the ear-tufts of the long-eared owl serve to easily distinguish the two (although long-eared owls can sometimes hold their ear-tufts flat). The iris-colour differs: yellow in the short-eared, and (often, though not always) orange in the long-eared, and the black surrounding the eyes is vertical on the long-eared, and horizontal on the short-eared. Overall the short-eared tends to be a paler, sandier bird than the long-eared. There are a number of other ways in which the two species the differ which are best seen when they are flying: a) the short-eared often has a broad white band along the rear edge of the wing, which is not shown by the long-eared; b) on the upperwing, short-eared owls' primary-patches are usually paler and more obvious; c) the band on the upper side of the short-eared owl's tail are usually bolder than those of the long-eared; d) the short-eared's innermost secondaries are often dark-marked, contrasting with the rest of the underwing; e) the long-eared owl has streaking throughout its underparts, whereas on the short-eared the streaking ends at the breast; f) the dark markings on the underside of the tips of the longest primaries are bolder on the short-eared owl; g) the upper parts are coarsely blotched, whereas on the long-eared they are more finely marked. The short-eared owl also differs structurally from the long-eared, having longer, slimmer wings: the long-eared owl has wings shaped more like those of a tawny owl. The long-eared owl generally has different habitat preferences from the short-eared, most often being found concealed in areas with dense wooded thickets. The short-eared owl is often most regularly seen flying about in early morning or late day as it hunts over open habitats.


Distribution and habitat

The short-eared owl occurs on all continents except Antarctica and Australia; thus it has one of the most widespread distributions of any bird. A. flammeus breeds in Europe, Asia, North and South America, the Caribbean, Hawaii and the Galápagos Islands. It is partially migratory, moving south in winter from the northern parts of its range. The short-eared owl is known to relocate to areas of higher rodent populations. It will also wander nomadically in search of better food supplies during years when vole populations are low. (See a map of the short-eared owl's distribution across the New World.)


Behaviour


Breeding

Sexual maturity is attained at one year. Breeding season in the northern hemisphere lasts from March to June, peaking in April. During this time these owls may gather in flocks. During breeding season, the males make great spectacles of themselves in flight to attract females. The male swoops down over the nest flapping its wings in a courtship display. These owls are generally monogamous.
The short-eared owl nests on the ground in prairie, tundra, savanna, or meadow habitats. Nests are concealed by low vegetation, and may be lightly lined by weeds, grass, or feathers. Approximately 4 to 7 white eggs are found in a typical clutch, but clutch size can reach up to a dozen eggs in years when voles are abundant. There is one brood per year. The eggs are incubated mostly by the female for 21–37 days. Offspring fledge at a little over four weeks. This owl is known to lure predators away from its nest by appearing to have a crippled wing.


Food and feeding

Hunting occurs mostly at night, but this owl is known to be diurnal and crepuscular as well. Its daylight hunting seems to coincide with the high-activity periods of voles, its preferred prey. It tends to fly only feet above the ground in open fields and grasslands until swooping down upon its prey feet-first. Several owls may hunt over the same open area. Its food consists mainly of rodents, especially voles, but it will eat other small mammals such as rabbits, mice, ground squirrels, shrews, rats, bats, muskrats and moles. It will also occasionally depredate smaller birds, especially when near sea-coasts and adjacent wetlands at which time they attack shorebirds, terns and small gulls and seabirds with semi-regularity. Avian prey is more infrequently preyed on inland and centers on passerines such as larks, icterids, starlings, tyrant flycatchers and pipits. Insects supplement the diet and short-eared owls may prey on roaches, grasshoppers, beetles, katydids and caterpillars. Competition can be fierce in North America with the northern harrier, with which the owl shares similar habitat and prey preferences. Both species will readily harass the other when prey is caught.
Because of the high pH in the stomach of owls they have a reduced ability to digest bone and other hard parts, so they eject pellets containing the remains of their prey.


Conservation status
The short-eared owl has an estimated global population of 1,200,000 to 2,100,000 and a very large range. On that basis, it is evaluated by the IUCN as a species of least concern.
It is listed as declining in the southern portion of its United States range. It is common in the northern portion of its breeding range. It is listed as endangered in New Mexico. Its appearance at the Calverton Executive Airpark on Long Island has prompted the New York State Department of Environmental Conservation to take the lead on ruling whether a massive redevelopment of the airport will receive the necessary environmental permits.


Gallery


References


Identification
Davis, A.H.; Prytherch, R.J. (1976). "Field identification of Long-eared and Short-eared Owls". British Birds. 69: 281–287.
Kemp, J.B. (1982). "Field identification of Long-eared and Short-eared Owls". British Birds. 75 (5): 227.
Robertson, Iain S. (1982). "Field identification of Long-eared and Short-eared Owls". British Birds. 75 (5): 227–229.
Kemp, J.B. (1982). "Tail-lengths of Long-eared and Short-eared Owls". British Birds. 75 (5): 230.

Note the choice of short, shady trees to roost under.]] The short-eared owl occurs on all continents except [[Antarctica]] and [[Australia]]; thus it has one of the most widespread distributions of any bird. ''A. flammeus'' breeds in [[Europe]], [[Asia]], [[North America|North]] and [[South America]], the [[Caribbean]], [[Hawaii]] and the [[Galápagos Islands]]. It is partially [[bird migration|migratory]], moving south in winter from the northern parts of its range. The short-eared owl is known to relocate to areas of higher rodent populations. It will also wander nomadically in search of better food supplies during years when [[vole]] populations are low. Breeding season in the northern hemisphere lasts from March to June, peaking in April. During this time these owls may gather in flocks. During breeding season, the males make great spectacles of themselves in flight to attract females. The male swoops down over the nest flapping its wings in a courtship display. These owls are generally [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]]. The short-eared owl nests on the ground in prairie, tundra, savanna, or meadow [[Habitat (ecology)|habitats]]. Nests are concealed by low vegetation, and may be lightly lined by weeds, grass, or feathers. Approximately 4 to 7 white eggs are found in a typical [[Clutch (eggs)|clutch]], but clutch size can reach up to a dozen eggs in years when [[vole]]s are abundant. There is one [[offspring|brood]] per year. The eggs are incubated mostly by the female for 21–37 days. Offspring [[fledge]] at a little over four weeks. This owl is known to lure predators away from its nest by appearing to have a crippled wing. ===Food and feeding=== [[File:Heringsmöwe (larus fuscus) greift Sumpfohreule (asio flammeus) an (Spiekeroog - Nationalpark niedersächsisches Wattenmeer) (2).jpg|thumb|[[Lesser black-backed gull]] chasing a short-eared owl]] Hunting occurs mostly at night, but this owl is known to be [[Diurnal animal|diurnal]] and [[crepuscular]] as well. Its daylight hunting seems to coincide with the high-activity periods of [[vole]]s, its preferred prey. It tends to fly only feet above the ground in open fields and grasslands until swooping down upon its prey feet-first. Several owls may hunt over the same open area. Its food consists mainly of [[rodents]], especially [[vole]]s, but it will eat other small [[mammal]]s such as [[rabbit]]s,{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/owls/short-eared-owl | title=Short-eared Owl | the Peregrine Fund }} [[mouse|mice]], [[ground squirrel]]s, [[shrew]]s, [[rat]]s, [[bat]]s, [[muskrat]]s and [[Mole (animal)|mole]]s. It will also occasionally depredate smaller birds, especially when near sea-coasts and adjacent wetlands at which time they attack [[shorebird]]s, [[tern]]s and small [[gull]]s and [[seabird]]s with semi-regularity. Avian prey is more infrequently preyed on inland and centers on passerines such as [[lark]]s, [[icterid]]s, [[starling]]s, [[tyrant flycatcher]]s and [[pipit]]s. [[Insect]]s supplement the diet and short-eared owls may prey on [[Cockroach|roach]]es, [[grasshopper]]s, [[beetle]]s, [[katydid]]s and [[caterpillar]]s. Competition can be fierce in [[North America]] with the [[northern harrier]], with which the owl shares similar habitat and prey preferences. Both species will readily harass the other when prey is caught. Because of the high pH in the stomach of owls they have a reduced ability to digest bone and other hard parts, so they eject pellets containing the remains of their prey. ==Conservation status== The short-eared owl has an estimated global population of 1,200,000 to 2,100,000 and a very large range. On that basis, it is evaluated by the [[IUCN]] as a species of [[least concern]]. It is listed as declining in the southern portion of its United States range. It is common in the northern portion of its breeding range. It is listed as endangered in [[New Mexico]]. zh.extract:
短耳鸮（學名：Asio flammeus），俗稱短耳猫头鹰，是一種廣泛分佈於草地的鴟鴞科（Strigidae）成員。屬於耳鴞屬（Asio）的鴞類被稱為耳鴞，因為它們有類似哺乳動物耳朵的羽毛簇。這些「耳」簇可能會顯現也可能不會顯現。短耳鴞在處於防禦姿態時會展示其羽毛簇，但由於其非常短小的羽毛簇通常不容易看到。短耳鴞主要棲息於開闊的鄉村和草地。


分類學
短耳鴞於1763年由路德教派主教埃里克·彭托皮丹正式描述，並被賦予了二名法名稱Strix flammea。 特定的種小名來自拉丁語，意思是「火焰色的」或「火焰般的」。 現今，這種鴞與其他七個物種一起歸入由法國動物學家馬蒂蘭·雅克·布里松於1760年引入的耳鴞屬（Asio）中。
認可的11個亞種如下:

A. f. flammeus – （Pontoppidan, 1763）：指名亞種，分佈於北美洲、歐洲、北非和北亞
A. f. cubensis – Garrido, 2007：分佈於古巴
A. f. domingensis – （Statius Müller, 1776）：分佈於伊斯帕尼奧拉島
A. f. portoricensis – Ridgway, 1882：分佈於波多黎各
A. f. bogotensis – Chapman, 1915：分佈於哥倫比亞、厄瓜多爾和秘魯西北部
A. f. galapagoensis – （Gould, 1837）：分佈於加拉帕戈斯群島
A. f. pallidicaudus – Friedmann, 1949：分佈於委內瑞拉、圭亞那和蘇里南
A. f. suinda – （Vieillot, 1817）：分佈於從秘魯南部和巴西南部到火地群島
A. f. sanfordi – Bangs, 1919：分佈於福克蘭群島
A. f. sandwichensis – （A. Bloxam, 1827）：Pueo'或夏威夷短耳鴞 - 分佈於夏威夷群島
A. f. ponapensis – Mayr, 1933：分佈於加羅林群島的波納佩島


描述
短耳鴞是一種中型鴞，身長約34—43 cm（13—17英寸），體重約206—475 g（7.3—16.8 oz）。 它具有大眼睛、大頭、短頸和寬大的翅膀。其喙短小、強壯且彎曲，呈黑色。其羽毛呈斑駁的黃褐色至棕色，尾部和翅膀有條紋。上胸部顯著有條紋。 它的飛行特徵是因不規則的振翅而顯得鬆散。短耳鴞的飛行也可能被描述為「像飛蛾或蝙蝠一樣」。 翼展範圍為85至110 cm（33至43英寸）。 雌性稍微大於雄性。A. flammeus的黃橙色眼睛被黑色環圈環繞，給人一種它們戴著睫毛膏的感覺，並且有大片的白色羽毛盤圍繞雙眼，像是戴著面具。


鳴聲
短耳鴞有著沙啞的吠叫聲。常見的聲音包括粗糙的waowk、waowk、waowk 或 toot-toot-toot-toot-toot。在繁殖地還能聽到響亮的eeee-yerp聲。然而，在越冬地短耳鴞通常是沉默的。


與長耳鴞的區別
在其大部分分布範圍內，短耳鴞與外貌相似的長耳鴞共同出現。在休息時，長耳鴞的耳簇使其很容易與短耳鴞區分開來（儘管長耳鴞有時會將耳簇壓平）。虹膜的顏色不同：短耳鴞是黃色，而長耳鴞是橙色，且長耳鴞的眼周黑色部分是垂直的，而短耳鴞是水平的。整體來說，短耳鴞的顏色通常比長耳鴞更加淺淡、沙色。這兩種鴞在飛行時有其他一些明顯的區別：a） 短耳鴞的翼後緣經常有一條寬闊的白色帶，而長耳鴞沒有；b） 在上翼，短耳鴞的初級羽毛斑塊通常更淺，更明顯；c） 短耳鴞尾巴上側的帶狀紋路通常比長耳鴞更加顯著；d） 短耳鴞最內側的次級羽毛經常帶有深色標記，與其餘的下翼形成對比；e） 長耳鴞的下體部分全都有條紋，而短耳鴞的條紋僅到胸部為止；f） 短耳鴞最長的初級羽毛下側尖端的深色標記比長耳鴞更為顯著；g） 短耳鴞的上部有粗大的斑塊，而長耳鴞的斑塊則更為細緻。短耳鴞的結構上也與長耳鴞不同，其翅膀更長、更瘦：長耳鴞的翅膀形狀更像灰林鴞。 長耳鴞的棲地偏好通常也與短耳鴞不同，通常隱藏在有濃密樹叢的地區。短耳鴞經常在清晨或黃昏時被看到，它們在開闊的棲地上空獵食。


分布與棲地

短耳鴞分布於除南極洲和澳大利亞外的所有大陸，因此它是分布最廣泛的鳥類之一。短耳鴞在歐洲、亞洲、北美洲、南美洲、加勒比地区、夏威夷和加拉帕戈斯群島繁殖。它具有部分的遷徙習性，冬季時會從分布範圍的北部遷徙至南方。短耳鴞已知會遷移到鼠類數量較多的地區。 在田鼠數量較少的年份，它們還會游牧性地尋找更豐富的食物供應。（請參閱短耳鴞在新大陸的分布圖。）


行為


繁殖
  
  
性成熟在一歲時達成。北半球的繁殖季節從三月持續到六月，四月達到高峰。在此期間，這些鴞可能會聚集成群。在繁殖季節，雄鴞會在飛行中展示其壯觀的求偶表演，以吸引雌鴞。雄鴞會在巢上方俯衝並拍打翅膀來進行求偶展示。 這些鴞通常是單配偶制的。
短耳鴞在草原、苔原、稀樹草原或草甸棲地的地面築巢。巢穴由低矮的植被隱蔽，可能會用雜草、草或羽毛稍微襯墊。 一般巢中有4到7枚白色的蛋，但在田鼠數量豐富的年份，巢中的蛋數量可達到十二枚。每年有一個巢。蛋主要由雌鴞孵化，孵化期為21至37天。後代在約四週齡時羽化。這種鴞已知會通過裝作翅膀受傷來引誘掠食者遠離巢穴。


食物與覓食
  
狩獵主要在夜間進行，但這種鴞也被認為是晝行性動物和曙暮性動物。它的日間狩獵似乎與田鼠這種首選獵物的高活動時期相吻合。 它通常在開闊的田野和草地上方僅幾英尺的高度飛行，直到俯衝而下用腳先抓住獵物。 幾隻鴞可能會在同一片開闊區域狩獵。 它的食物主要由囓齒動物，特別是田鼠組成，但它也會吃其他小型哺乳動物，例如兔子、 老鼠、地松鼠、鼩鼱、大鼠、蝙蝠、麝鼠和鼴鼠。偶爾它也會捕食小型鳥類，特別是在靠近海岸和濕地附近時，它們會定期攻擊鷸鴴類、燕鷗、小型海鷗和海鳥。在內陸地區，鳥類獵物較少見，主要集中在雀形目，如雲雀、擬黃鸝科、椋鳥科、霸鶲和鷚。昆蟲是其飲食的補充，短耳鴞可能會捕食蟑螂、蚱蜢、甲蟲、螽斯和毛毛蟲。在北美，它與北方鷹鴞之間的競爭可能非常激烈，因為兩者共享相似的棲地和獵物偏好。當捕到獵物時，兩種物種都會樂於騷擾對方。
由於鴞的胃內酸鹼值較高，它們消化骨頭和其他硬質部分的能力降低，因此會排出包含獵物殘骸的食塊。


保育狀況
短耳鴞的全球估計種群數量在120萬到210萬之間，且分布範圍非常廣泛。基於此，IUCN將其評估為無危物種。
在美國分布範圍的南部地區，其數量被列為下降。在其繁殖範圍的北部地區則很常見。 在新墨西哥州被列為瀕危物種。其出現在長島的卡弗頓行政機場促使紐約州環境保護局率先裁定機場的龐大重建項目是否能獲得必要的環境許可。
